sources.title: Export and import using pg_dump, pg_dumpall, and pg_restore

claims.summary: Google documents Cloud SQL as a managed relational database service with PostgreSQL editions.
limitations: No Cloud SQL project, instance or control-plane configuration was inspected.

claims.summary: Google's version policy lists PostgreSQL 16 as supported by Cloud SQL.
limitations: The exact edition, minor version and London machine availability were not queried.

claims.summary: Google's Cloud SQL locations page lists europe-west2 as London.
limitations: Instance location alone does not settle backup location or international transfer obligations.

claims.summary: Google documents Cloud SQL customer data encryption at rest.
limitations: No key hierarchy, CMEK option or configured instance was inspected.

claims.summary: Google documents encryption of Cloud SQL backups as part of its data-at-rest controls.
limitations: No backup metadata, encryption evidence or restored target was inspected.

claims.summary: Google documents point-in-time recovery for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances.
limitations: No transaction-boundary RPO, log continuity or recovery gap was measured.

claims.summary: Google documents restoring backups or PITR into a new Cloud SQL instance, including another project or region in supported cases.
limitations: Alpacaly target-isolation and empty-target checks were not exercised against Cloud SQL.

claims.summary: Google documents SSL/TLS, CA validation and server identity verification options for Cloud SQL.
limitations: No enforced SSL mode, CA hierarchy, DNS name or verify-full client connection was inspected.

claims.summary: Google documents private IP connectivity through private services access for Cloud SQL.
limitations: Public IP disablement, VPC routing, DNS and firewall controls were not configured or tested.

claims.summary: Google documents pg_dump, pg_dumpall and pg_restore workflows for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL.
limitations: Alpacaly extensions, roles, triggers and complete export portability were not tested.

claims.summary: Google publishes Cloud SQL instance monitoring through Cloud Monitoring metrics and dashboards.
limitations: No Alpacaly alert policy, route, threshold, delivery or on-call response was configured.

claims.summary: The reviewed material establishes managed service and networking boundaries, but does not prove Alpacaly's separate application, migration, backup, restore and monitoring roles.
limitations: No IAM policy, database role, service account, break-glass control or role audit was inspected.

claims.summary: This candidate review did not establish the exact Alpacaly secret-storage and rotation design.
limitations: General Google Secret Manager integration was outside this Cloud SQL candidate evidence review.

claims.summary: Google publishes monitoring capabilities, but this review did not prove complete immutable administrative audit coverage for the proposed design.
limitations: No Cloud Audit Logs export, retention rule or identity-backed reviewer evidence was inspected.

claims.summary: Google publishes Cloud SQL backup and retention capabilities.
limitations: Required daily, weekly, monthly, minimum-retention and deletion-evidence policy was not configured.

claims.summary: The reviewed Cloud SQL documents did not establish an approved legal or incident hold workflow for Alpacaly backups.
limitations: The existing Alpacaly hold registry still requires an approved provider-side operating procedure.

claims.summary: This review did not record the applicable London quotas, connection limits, storage bounds or restore constraints for the proposed edition and size.
limitations: Edition, project quota and machine-specific limits require a later controlled review.

claims.summary: No decision-grade incident-history review was completed for this candidate.
limitations: A public status view is not equivalent to contractual or candidate-specific incident evidence.

claims.summary: Google publishes customer-care information, but no plan was selected and no response commitment was accepted.
limitations: Entitlement, response target, escalation route and cost require independent contractual review.

claims.summary: Google publishes variable Cloud SQL pricing components, but this review does not contain an approved London architecture or monthly quote.
limitations: Edition, compute, storage, backup, network, monitoring, support and tax assumptions remain unpriced.

claims.summary: Google documents a London region for Cloud SQL, but this is not a completed UK data-transfer or privacy assessment.
limitations: Backup locations, support access, telemetry, subprocessors and contractual transfers remain unreviewed.

claims.summary: Published Google documentation cannot prove Alpacaly recovery-safety mode, claim fencing, emergency-stop preservation or OUTCOME_UNKNOWN handling.
limitations: A disposable provider-backed restore drill is mandatory before any later staging evaluation can pass.

openQuestions: Which London edition and deployment shape meets the proposed RPO and RTO without relying on untested assumptions?
How will separate application, migration, backup, restore and monitoring identities be enforced and audited?
What backup, PITR and telemetry data can leave the UK region under normal operations or support access?
What customer-care tier, escalation route and response commitment would be contractually available?
What are the full monthly costs for HA, backups, PITR, monitoring, transfer, support and restore drills?
How will legal holds and deletion evidence be applied without weakening the existing append-only catalogue?
